cards.title: Storage Is The Substrate
cards.teaser: Other lifecycle stages are verbs; storage is the noun underneath all of them. Picking storage well is the highest-leverage architectural decision you'll make.

runs.text: Every other stage is something you do
to
storage — ingestion writes to it, transformation reads-and-writes it, serving reads from it. Storage shapes:

items.text: Columnar warehouses make analytical queries fast that would take hours on row stores.
Storing petabytes in a warehouse vs in object storage differs by 10× in cost.
Schema changes in some stores are migrations; in others they're metadata operations.
What can be queried in parallel.
Distributed storage scales reads; single-node doesn't.

runs.text: A pipeline running on the wrong storage shape will struggle at every stage. A pipeline on the right storage shape feels almost too easy.
The data engineer's job is matching storage shape to access pattern at every layer of the stack — and accepting that "one storage system for everything" is rarely the right answer at scale.
When debugging slow pipelines, the storage layer is the first place to look. Most performance problems trace back to a mismatch between how data is stored and how it's queried.
Same role as memory hierarchy in a CPU — the speed and shape of the underlying medium shapes everything that runs on top.

cards.title: Raw Materials — Disk, Memory, Network
cards.teaser: All storage abstractions ultimately ride on physical hardware with very different speeds, costs, and access shapes. Knowing the underlying primitives clarifies the trade-offs above.

runs.text: The physical layer:

items.text: — nanosecond access, gigabytes capacity, expensive per byte, lost on power off.
— microsecond access, terabytes capacity, moderate cost, persistent.
— millisecond access, terabytes-cheap, large capacity, persistent.
— tens of milliseconds access, effectively unlimited capacity, very cheap, persistent and durable.

runs.text: Each is six orders of magnitude apart in speed. Pricing inverts the order — RAM costs 100-1000× more per GB than HDD; HDD costs 5-10× more than object storage.
The implication for data systems:
stored data wants to live cheaply; queried data wants to be near the compute
. Storage tiers move data toward cheaper media as it cools off (rarely accessed → archive tier on S3 Glacier).
Modern warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery) are essentially fancy abstractions that put hot data in fast tiers and cold data in slow tiers automatically — and bill you for the journey.
When a query is slow, ask "where does the data physically live?" before tuning the query. Sometimes the right fix is moving the data, not changing the SQL.
Same hierarchy idea as caches in a CPU — L1 to L2 to L3 to RAM to disk, six orders of magnitude across the stack.

cards.title: Object Storage — The Substrate Of The Modern Data Lake
cards.teaser: S3 and its peers are now the default place to land raw data. Cheap, durable, infinitely scalable, and the foundation of most modern data architectures.

runs.text: Object storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob) is the most important data infrastructure of the past decade. The defining properties:

items.text: — 11 nines for S3 means you essentially won't lose data.
— pennies per GB-month, with cheaper tiers for cold data.
— every tool and framework supports it.
— used to be the gotcha; mostly resolved as of S3's strong consistency in 2020.

runs.text: What it gave us:

items.text: — store everything cheaply and figure out structure later.
— different engines (Spark, Presto, Trino, warehouses) can read the same files.
— Iceberg, Delta, Hudi turn object storage into something with warehouse-like semantics.

runs.text: What it costs:

items.text: — every PUT/GET is metered; lots of small files create surprise bills.
— tens of milliseconds per request; not a substitute for an OLTP database.
— listing a bucket with millions of objects is slow.

runs.text: When designing object-storage layouts, partition by query pattern. Date-partitioned files (
year=2026/month=05/day=02/
) let queries skip directories instead of scanning everything.
Same role as Linux's filesystem in a hosted-service form — the universal substrate every other tool can read and write.

cards.title: Row Storage Versus Column Storage
cards.teaser: Two fundamentally different ways to physically arrange data. Row stores favor transactional access; column stores favor analytical queries. The wrong choice tanks performance.

runs.text: Row storage.

All values for a single row live together on disk. Reading row 47's email means reading row 47's entire record. Optimal when you read whole rows: OLTP databases (Postgres, MySQL) put one row's worth of data per record.
Column storage.

All values for a single column live together. Reading the email column means reading just that column's contiguous bytes for many rows. Optimal when you scan many rows but few columns: analytical queries (
SELECT email FROM users WHERE ...
The implications:

items.text: — column storage compresses dramatically better. A column of repeated countries (US, US, US, UK, US) compresses to almost nothing; the row form interleaves countries with ages and emails.
— analytical queries on column storage scan only the columns they need. Row storage reads everything.
— row storage handles single-row inserts cleanly. Column storage prefers batch writes; per-row inserts are inefficient.

runs.text: The split:
This is one reason transactional databases make poor analytical engines, and vice versa.
If your "warehouse" is built on a row store and queries scan whole tables, you have an architecture mismatch. The fix is migrating to a columnar engine, not tuning the queries.
Same idea as struct-of-arrays vs array-of-structs in low-level programming — choose the layout that matches how you'll iterate.

cards.title: File Formats — Parquet, ORC, Avro, Delta
cards.teaser: How data is encoded on disk matters as much as where it sits. Modern formats are the difference between fast queries and pipelines that crawl.

runs.text: The major formats and their roles:

items.text: — text-based, universal, slow, no schema. Use for human-readable export and not much else.
— text-based, schemaless, easy to debug, expensive to query. Common for API output and event streams.
— row-based binary, schema-embedded, good for streaming sources where each record is processed individually. Used heavily with Kafka.
— column-based binary, best-in-class compression, the default for analytical workloads. Read by every major engine.
— columnar, similar to Parquet, popular in Hive/Hadoop ecosystems.
on top of Parquet that add ACID, schema evolution, time travel.

runs.text: The hierarchy in 2026:

items.text: → Avro for the event payload, Parquet for derived analytical tables.
→ Parquet for analytical workloads; original format (often JSON) for replayability.
→ Iceberg or Delta as the table format wrapping Parquet.
→ whatever the database uses internally; not your concern.

runs.text: If you're still landing CSV in your data lake in 2026, your queries are 5-10× slower than they could be and you're paying for it on every scan.
Same idea as choosing between MP3 and WAV — both store audio, one's an order of magnitude smaller for the same content.

cards.title: Compression — Free Speed, Mostly
cards.teaser: Most modern formats compress data heavily before writing. Right compression choice means smaller storage bills and faster scans.

runs.text: Compression buys you two things in data systems: smaller storage cost and less data to scan (which means faster queries). Common algorithms:

items.text: — fast compress/decompress, moderate ratio. Default for many engines because it's fast enough that decompression doesn't slow queries.
— slow compress, good ratio. Common for archive data, less common for hot tables.
— extremely fast, modest ratio. Good when CPU is the bottleneck.
— newer, tunable compression level, often the best modern default. Good ratio at high speed.
— high ratio, slow compress. Good for cold archive data.

runs.text: Plus columnar-specific encodings within the format:

items.text: — replace string values with integer IDs into a dictionary. Country codes, status enums, anything with finite cardinality.
— store small integers in fewer bits than a full int.

runs.text: You don't usually pick these manually — Parquet writers choose per-column. But understanding what's happening explains why a 10TB raw dataset becomes a 200GB Parquet table.
Compression ratio of 50× is normal on well-shaped analytical data. If your Parquet files are barely smaller than the source CSV, something's wrong with your encoding.
Same magic as gzipping log files — ten lines that all start with
compress almost to nothing.

cards.title: Open Table Formats — The Lakehouse Pattern
cards.teaser: Iceberg, Delta, and Hudi turn directories of Parquet files into something with warehouse semantics: ACID, schema evolution, time travel, snapshot isolation.

runs.text: The problem they solve: a directory of Parquet files in S3 is a great storage primitive but a terrible
table
. You can't safely append or update without race conditions. Schema changes are coordinated by hand. There's no concept of a "current version" of the table.
Open table formats add a metadata layer that manages all of this:

items.text: — multiple writers can append safely; readers see consistent snapshots.
— add, drop, rename, change-type columns without rewriting all the files.
— query the table as of a previous snapshot. Roll back if a bad write happens.
— partitioning is metadata; you can change partition layout without rewriting data.
— merge small files into larger ones in the background.

runs.text: The three players:

items.text: — Netflix-originated, vendor-neutral, the broadest engine support.
— Databricks-originated, recently open-sourced, deep Spark integration.
— Uber-originated, good at streaming-style upserts and CDC ingestion.

runs.text: The lakehouse pattern (Databricks coined it; Iceberg + warehouses now embody it) is converging warehouse and lake into one architecture: cheap object storage, open formats, multiple engines reading the same tables.
If you're starting fresh in 2026, Iceberg or Delta on object storage is the safest forward-looking bet. The path from there to either a warehouse or pure lake compute is well-trodden.
Same arc as filesystems — POSIX gave us shared semantics over raw disks; table formats give us shared semantics over object storage.

cards.title: Cloud Warehouses — Managed Analytical Databases
cards.teaser: Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Databricks SQL — purpose-built for analytical queries at scale, with the operational burden taken off your team.

runs.text: Cloud warehouses are the dominant analytical storage choice. The defining capabilities:

items.text: — queries fan out across many nodes.
— independent scaling of each. Warm storage in the warehouse vendor's storage; spin up compute clusters as needed.
— every analyst tool can query without custom drivers.
— backups, replication, security, scaling all handled.

runs.text: The major options:

items.text: — vendor-neutral, multi-cloud, great UX, premium pricing.
— Google's offering, serverless model, strong ML integration.
— AWS's, deeply integrated with the AWS ecosystem, cluster-management can be operational work.
— built on top of the Databricks platform, integrates closely with Spark and Delta.

runs.text: The trade-off: you get a tremendous amount of capability for the operational cost of zero (no clusters to manage). The bill grows with usage; the lock-in is real (proprietary SQL extensions, native data types). Most teams accept the trade-off because the alternative is operating Spark or Trino themselves.
Warehouses' biggest cost driver is usually compute on poorly-optimized queries. Spend time on partitioning, clustering, and query tuning before adding more compute.
Same shape as managed Postgres vs running your own — pay more for the service, save the operational time.

cards.title: Data Lakes — Flexible But Demanding
cards.teaser: A data lake is what you get when you put raw data on object storage and decide what shape it should take later. Cheap, flexible, and easy to turn into a swamp if no one's tending it.

runs.text: A data lake is less a product and more a pattern: object storage holding raw data, compute engines (Spark, Presto, Trino) reading it. The wins:

items.text: — object storage costs almost nothing per TB.
— anything from CSV to images to JSON to Parquet.
— different teams can use different engines on the same data.
— petabytes of training data, raw event streams, regulatory archives.

runs.text: The losses (without discipline):

items.text: — what data exists where? Catalogs become essential.
— no schema enforcement means anything can land; consumers waste time validating.
— who has access to what? Permissions on raw object storage are coarse.
— without partitioning and good formats, queries scan terabytes for trivial results.

runs.text: The "data swamp" anti-pattern: a lake that grew without discipline, where finding the right dataset takes longer than recreating it from scratch.
What turns a lake into a useful asset:

items.text: — bronze (raw), silver (cleaned), gold (curated) layers with progressive refinement.
— Iceberg or Delta to add structure.
— a catalog (Glue, Unity Catalog, Polaris) tracking what's where.

runs.text: "We have a data lake" too often means "we have a few hundred TB of files no one understands." Lakes need governance and curation to be useful, not just storage.
Same as a hard drive shared by a team without folders — works briefly, then nobody can find anything.

cards.title: The Lakehouse Convergence
cards.teaser: Lakes are gaining warehouse features. Warehouses are gaining lake features. The line between them keeps blurring, and most modern stacks blend both.

runs.text: For years, lakes and warehouses had clear roles. Lakes were cheap raw storage; warehouses were expensive analytics-optimized engines. The gap is closing fast:
From the lake side, gaining warehouse features:

items.text: Iceberg / Delta tables with ACID, schema evolution, time travel.
High-performance query engines (Trino, Presto, Spark SQL, DuckDB) that scan lake-format files efficiently.
Catalogs and governance layers (Unity Catalog, Polaris) that match warehouse-style metadata.

runs.text: From the warehouse side, gaining lake features:

items.text: Snowflake's external tables and Iceberg support.
BigQuery's BigLake reading lake-format files directly.
Databricks SQL serving Delta Lake tables.

runs.text: The convergence point — the lakehouse — combines:

items.text: Object storage as the physical substrate (cheap, scalable).
Multiple compute engines (analytical SQL, ML training, batch processing).

runs.text: The practical implication: in 2026 you can often pick a single architecture (lakehouse) instead of running both a lake and a warehouse. Whether to do so depends on your specific access patterns and team skills.

If your warehouse spend is dominated by storage and your lake is mostly serving warehouse-style queries, you have two architectures doing one job. Consolidating saves money and operational complexity.
Same as Linux desktop and macOS converging on similar UX — different starting points, same end-state pressures.

cards.title: OLTP, OLAP, And When To Use Each
cards.teaser: Two acronyms that draw the most important boundary in data system design: transactional vs analytical workloads.

runs.text: OLTP — Online Transaction Processing.

Optimized for many small, low-latency reads and writes. Application databases. Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, DynamoDB. Workload shape: "read this user's profile," "insert this order," "update this inventory count." Latency target: single-digit milliseconds.
OLAP — Online Analytical Processing.

Optimized for fewer large reads that scan and aggregate. Data warehouses, lakes. Snowflake, BigQuery, Spark, ClickHouse. Workload shape: "show me total revenue by country by month for the last year." Latency target: seconds to minutes is fine.
The contrast in physical design:

items.text: — OLTP milliseconds; OLAP seconds to minutes.
— OLTP high (thousands of small queries); OLAP lower (tens of large queries).
— OLTP in-place; OLAP append or recompute.
— OLTP primary + many secondaries; OLAP sort/cluster keys.
— OLTP few rows via indexed lookups; OLAP many rows via hash or merge joins.

runs.text: The error to avoid: running analytical queries against your OLTP database, or building user-facing applications on a warehouse. Each is fine for what it's designed for; both are bad at the other's job.

HTAP ("hybrid") systems exist (TiDB, SingleStore, CockroachDB with analytics extensions) but the OLTP/OLAP split remains the cleanest mental model. Most teams run separate systems and ETL between them.
Same as splitting hot-path code from batch jobs — different optimization targets, different physical layouts.

cards.title: Specialized Stores — Time Series, Vector, Search
cards.teaser: Beyond OLTP and OLAP, several specialized storage types serve specific workloads better than either generalist would.

runs.text: When workload patterns get specialized, specialized stores often beat the generalists:

items.text: (InfluxDB, Prometheus, TimescaleDB) — optimized for high-volume time-stamped data with downsampling, retention, range queries. Used for metrics, telemetry, IoT.
(Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Solr) — full-text indexing and ranked retrieval. Logs, product search, knowledge bases.
(Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector) — similarity search over high-dimensional embeddings. The default for RAG and recommendation use cases.
(Neo4j, JanusGraph) — relationships are first-class; traversal queries that would join 10 tables in SQL are native operations.
(Redis, DynamoDB, Cassandra) — millisecond reads on a primary key, often used as serving caches.
(MongoDB, Couchbase, Firestore) — schemaless JSON-style storage with flexible querying.

runs.text: The decision: if your access pattern matches a specialized store's sweet spot by 10×, run it. If your access is mostly mixed, generalists (Postgres for OLTP, a warehouse for OLAP) are usually simpler.
Each specialized store you add is a new operational burden — backups, monitoring, scaling, security. Adopt only when the access-pattern win clearly justifies the cost.
Same trade-off as picking specialized hardware (GPU, TPU) vs CPU — beats the generalist on its sweet spot, costs more in operational complexity.

cards.title: Storage Tiering — Hot, Warm, Cold
cards.teaser: Most data gets accessed once and never again. Tiering moves cold data to cheap storage automatically, cutting bills by 5-10× without affecting fresh queries.

runs.text: Data has a temperature curve. Today's data gets hammered; last year's data gets queried occasionally; data from five years ago is mostly archive. Storage tiers exploit this:

items.text: — fast, expensive. Recent data, frequently queried. Cloud SSDs, in-warehouse storage.
— slower, cheaper. Data accessed monthly. Standard object storage tiers.
— slowest, cheapest. Archive data, regulatory holds. S3 Glacier, Azure Archive.
— tape-equivalent. Multi-year retention you might never read. Deep Archive tiers.

runs.text: Per-GB-month cost ratios (rough): hot 1×, warm 0.5×, cold 0.05×, frozen 0.01×.
How to use tiering:

items.text: — move objects to cheaper tiers automatically based on age.
— keep cold data on object storage; query it occasionally without paying warehouse storage rates.
— drop or archive old partitions on a schedule.

runs.text: The retrieval cost: cold-tier reads are cheap per GB but have higher latency and per-request fees. Reading a single object back from Glacier can take hours. Plan tiering with retrieval needs in mind.
If your warehouse storage bill is large, ten minutes with the access pattern report (which tables are scanned monthly vs. weekly vs. daily) usually identifies cold candidates.
Same as the cache hierarchy — keep hot bytes in the expensive layer, push cold bytes down to cheap layers, profit on the average.

cards.title: Storage Costs Are Architectural, Not Just Operational
cards.teaser: How you structure storage drives a huge fraction of cloud data spend. Architectural decisions made early determine whether your storage bill is reasonable or alarming.

runs.text: Architectural decisions that drive storage cost:

items.text: — Parquet vs CSV is 5-10× compression. JSON vs Parquet is similar.
— well-partitioned data lets queries skip 99% of files. Poorly-partitioned data forces full scans.
— keep data forever and storage compounds. Aggressive retention windows cap the growth.
— copying the same data into a lake, a warehouse, and a feature store triples the bill.
— multi-region storage doubles or triples bills; sometimes necessary, often default-on without need.
— many small Parquet files bill for every PUT and slow down queries; periodic compaction is essential.

runs.text: Patterns that keep storage costs sane:

items.text: — raw lands once; downstream consumers read from there or from layered derivatives.
— every dataset has a documented retention; automated policies enforce it.
— everything analytical lands as Parquet (or in a table format). No raw JSON sitting in the warehouse.
— small files get merged into 100MB+ chunks regularly.
— queries that touch one day shouldn't scan all-time data.

runs.text: Storage costs grow with O(time × volume × replication). Each multiplier is something you can dial. Cost shock usually means at least one is on a default you didn't choose.
Same idea as software complexity — small architectural decisions early compound into either a manageable system or a permanent tax.
